# Questions in Livestream 252

Live Stream #252: The Golden Sound Fox

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=652" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:52</a> | What is the canon ending of Egg Hunter? | There is no single canon ending; the game has seven possible endings based on player choices. | He says Egg Hunter does not have one canonical ending. Instead, there are seven possible endings, and which one the player gets depends on choices made during the game. He also notes that he did the art, programming, and music, while Mrs. Producer wrote the story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:16</a> | Will David make a video game in 3D? | No. He said one video game per lifetime is enough and that a 3D project would require more energy, time, and infrastructure than he has for it. | He says he is not keen to make another game and does not have the energy, time, or infrastructure for a 3D game. He adds that he is not interested in starting a game company; if Mrs. Producer ever pursued that, she would handle the staffing and project work, not him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:23</a> | What language was Egg Hunter developed in? | It was developed in a mix of Ren'Py and Python. | He answers directly that Egg Hunter was built with a mix of Ren'Py and Python, describing it as essentially a Python-based development. He treats that as the answer to the game-technical questions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=906" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:06</a> | Are women more prone to heresy than men? | He said not as individuals in any simple sense, though he argued group dynamics can affect how error spreads. | He rejects the idea that women are individually more prone to heresy, but argues that people behave differently in groups. His distinction is that a group of women may be more tolerant or accepting of error because of group dynamics, while that is not the same as women as individuals being more heretical than men. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=1059" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:39</a> | In Exodus 14:22, could a wind have made the waters appear like a wall on either side? | He said `wall` should be read in its ancient sense and not imagined as vertical movie-style water towers. | He reads the wall image through ancient wall forms, especially a sloped temenos wall rather than a vertical building wall. On that reading, a wind setdown could push water aside so that it piles up like sloped banks, not like the tall, sharply cut water walls of film depictions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=1213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:13</a> | Would a name for `Ramesses, son of Ahmose` actually look like `Ramesses`? | No. He said the forms look similar at first glance but differ in an important grammatical element. | He distinguishes `Ra'mess`, meaning roughly Ra begot, from `Ra'messu`, meaning Ra begot him. The added pronoun makes `Ramesses` a different name, and he says confusing the two is an English-speaker problem that misses the Egyptian grammar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=1493" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:53</a> | How did you approach Exodus 32 to reach your conclusions about what is happening in the text? | He said he starts with the text itself, then compares it with cultural context and related passages before drawing conclusions. | He says he reads the text contextually: first what Exodus 32 says, then cultural context and related biblical euphemisms. He argues that the people's question about what became of Moses functions like a euphemistic `where is he` statement about death or nonexistence, so the golden calf episode should be read through that cultural and scriptural pattern rather than in isolation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=2035" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:55</a> | Is there any meaningful difference between resurrection and revivification? | If someone is truly dead, he said there is no practical difference in the basic claim that life has been restored. | He says that if the person is genuinely dead, there is no meaningful distinction for the basic claim: the dead person has been restored to life. He applies this to Jonah, arguing that attempts to make Jonah merely swoon are efforts to avoid the simpler reading that God raised him from death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=2056" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:16</a> | Do you think the Pharisees and Sadducees fought over Jonah and resurrection? | Yes. He said their different views of life after death and scripture would make that dispute likely. | He says the Pharisees believed in life after death while the Sadducees did not, and that the Sadducees accepted only the Pentateuch as scripture rather than Jonah or the prophets. On that basis, he thinks they would have disagreed over a Jonah resurrection reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=2287" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:07</a> | Why not consider calf worship from Mesopotamian and Canaanite religions as the source for Israel's golden-calf tradition? | He said Egypt is the more likely source in the Exodus setting, while Mesopotamian or Canaanite explanations often depend on non-Exodus assumptions. | He says calf and cow worship existed broadly, but the question is the most likely source for Israel in the narrative setting. He argues the Israelites had not been in Mesopotamian contact for centuries, were not yet in Canaan, and had clearer contact with Egyptian bovine cults such as Hathor at Avaris. He also says some Canaanite or Mesopotamian explanations follow infiltration or native-uprising models that he finds implausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=2505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:45</a> | Why insist the Exodus 32 idol had to be gold and golden in appearance? | He said Sinai supplied more than gold alone, but the golden-calf language still matters and should not be flattened away. | He rejects the premise that gold was the only available metal, saying the Israelites had silver and especially bronze as well. His point is that Egyptian idols could be bronze, silver, gold, or mixed-metal constructions, but Exodus still specifically presents a gold and golden calf, and that wording helps distinguish the idol from alternatives such as an Apis-bull explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=2639" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:59</a> | Were Egyptian gold idols alloyed like 10-karat gold mixed with other metals? | Not usually in that way. He said metals were generally kept separate and mechanically attached rather than melted together. | He says late Bronze Age gold was often roughly 14-karat because of cupellation, but Egyptians generally tried to keep metals separate. An idol could have a silver body with a gold head, for example, but those parts would be mechanically attached rather than melted together into a mixed alloy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=2726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:26</a> | Why do some scholars think Exodus 32 was written after Jeroboam's calf incident in 1 Kings 12? | He said they reverse the dependence because of critical assumptions, but he rejected that move and treated it as poor reasoning. | He links that view to late-dating assumptions and some forms of critical scholarship, then argues that 1 Kings itself is late enough that dependence can run the other way. He suggests Jeroboam or the Kings narrative may be echoing Exodus 32, or that scribal shaping may have highlighted the idolatrous parallel, rather than Exodus being invented from 1 Kings 12. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=3053" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:53</a> | Did the Egyptians have hammocks? | No. He said they could have made them, but the Egyptian environment did not naturally lend itself to the usual setup. | He says Egyptians could have made hammocks in principle, but the environment made them impractical because hammocks need suitable trees close together. Egypt had relatively few trees, often scrub species, so the normal hammock setup would not have been common or useful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=3107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:47</a> | What are the best books or commentaries for studying Isaiah? | He said there are many good options and named works by John Oswalt, John Goldingay, H. G. M. Williamson, Geoffrey Grogan, Edward Young, Barry Webb, and J. Alec Motyer. | He says Isaiah is unusually well served by commentaries compared with books such as Exodus, Revelation, or Chronicles. He lists multiple usable authors rather than a single best volume, emphasizing that good Isaiah commentaries are numerous and fairly easy to obtain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=3245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:05</a> | What are your views on the 1972 Toronto egregore experiment? | He dismissed it as methodologically flawed and ultimately inconclusive because it lacked proper controls and was not double blind. | He describes the experiment as an attempt to create or contact a fictional psychic entity named Philip through seance groups, then use that entity to pass information. He dismisses the idea and the experiment because the design lacked proper controls, was not double blind, produced contradictory information, and therefore could not establish the claimed result. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=3423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:03</a> | How much freedom did ordinary Egyptians have to leave their work or village? | He said very little; an ordinary farmer could not simply move away without a good reason and permission. | He says ordinary Egyptians had very little freedom of movement, and even a hereditary governor could not leave his territory without royal permission. The concern was often theft of royal property rather than rebellion, so movement was tightly controlled; even sailors on trade routes had limits on how far they could move from port areas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=3711" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:51</a> | Would Egyptian craftsmen have lived in guarded, sequestered villages, almost like a police state? | Many working specialists probably did live in controlled settlements, though he did not think every Egyptian lived under that exact pattern. | He says many working craftsmen, especially those handling valuable materials such as wood, metal, ivory, lapis, or jasper, would be in controlled settlements. He qualifies that some trades such as shoemaking or ordinary cloth work might not always be sequestered, but he still describes ancient Egypt as a military dictatorship with strong movement rules rather than a modern free society. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=3874" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:34</a> | What do you think about robots built for human companionship? | He called it a bad idea, arguing that artificial companions cannot replace real human relationships and may deepen relational distortion. | He says robot companionship creates a simulacrum relationship that lacks the nuance, disagreement, and pushback of real human relationships. He also raises an ethical concern: if a robot ever had some form of sentience, designing it for human companionship or sexual use would become exploitative rather than merely unwise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=4133" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:53</a> | If slaves were above farmers, could a slave bully a farmer, and would the slave's master represent him in court? | A slave with a powerful master's delegated authority could bully farmers, though the transcript does not clearly answer the court-representation part. | He says a slave could bully a farmer depending on the slave's status and the master's authority. For example, if a nomarch made a slave overseer of taxation, that slave could legally pressure farmers to extract taxes because he was acting under the nomarch's authority. He compares this to powerful Roman imperial slaves, but he does not give a direct answer about court representation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=4407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:27</a> | Are you a cessationist or a continuationist, and why? | He identified as a continuationist, holding that spiritual gifts continue beyond the apostolic age. | He defines a continuationist as someone who believes spiritual gifts continued after the apostles, while a cessationist believes they ceased with them. He says he does not accept cessationism because church history contains many reports of continuing gifts, and he also says he has experienced spiritual gifts himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=4475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:35</a> | Does Joshua 6:25's `to this day` wording mean Joshua was written before Rahab died? | Not necessarily. He said the phrase can mean `ever since`, while still allowing that Joshua may preserve material from around the early monarchy. | He argues that the phrase should be understood more like `ever since`, meaning Rahab and her household continued among Israel after Jericho rather than being abandoned there. He says the wording does not require Rahab to be alive when the book was written, and he notes that other features of Joshua may point to an early monarchic setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=4810" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:10</a> | Will we remember things from this life in heaven? | Yes, but he said those memories will lose their present weight and urgency in the fullness of eternal life. | He says people will remember this life, but those memories will become less important as eternal life continues in the glory of God. He compares it to remembering childhood events that are real memories but no longer carry the same weight later in life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=4952" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:32</a> | What do you think manna was during the wilderness years? | He suggested something like a fungus or desert truffle analogue rather than treating the description as random or purely symbolic. | He proposes a fungus similar to African truffle, which lives under the surface and fruits in wafer-like forms, often around dawn when dew triggers a response. Because he also thinks Sinai was savanna-like at the time, he sees such a fungus as a plausible natural analogue for the manna description. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=5060" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:20</a> | What is the best book for Egyptian foods and table etiquette? | He said table etiquette is not really the right category for most ancient Egyptians, but recommended Lise Manniche's work on Egyptian herbs and food. | He says ordinary Egyptians did not usually eat at tables because table furniture was expensive; most people reclined or ate from a shared platter. For food and herbs he recommends Lise Manniche's *An Egyptian Herbal*, while warning that there are not many strong books on Egyptian food as such. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=5225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:05</a> | What is the best book on Egyptian symbols and symbolism? | He said the topic is too broad for one dependable book and should be narrowed to particular symbols. | He says symbolism in general is too broad for one useful recommendation because symbols vary by object, culture, and context. He suggests narrowing the question to particular symbols; otherwise a general book recommendation would be unreliable or too unfocused. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=5843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:23</a> | When did humans start cooking food? | He said humans probably started cooking as soon as they started using fire. | He treats the answer as straightforward: when humans began using fire, they began cooking food. He mentions fire pits around 50,000 to 80,000 years ago and possible fire remains from much earlier, so he gives a broad ancient range rather than a precise date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=6133" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:13</a> | What are spiritual gifts? | He described them as abilities given by God to believers for the service and strengthening of the church. | He describes spiritual gifts as gifts given when someone becomes one with Christ and intended for use in the church. His examples include prophecy, tongues, interpretation of tongues, discernment, hospitality, and administration, stressing that the category is broader than speaking in tongues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=6523" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:43</a> | Do you need goat stomach acid to make cheese? | No. He said rennet is an enzyme from the duodenum, not stomach acid. | He explains that cheesemaking uses rennet, which he describes as an enzyme or protein from the lining of the duodenum rather than actual stomach acid. He adds that rennet is easy to buy from cheesemaking suppliers or even Amazon, and that plant-based rennet exists though he does not think it works as well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=6637" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:37</a> | What is the best cheese? | He said it depends on the person, but named a favorite cheese the transcript renders as `Schloss` or `Scharfe`. | He says the best cheese depends on who is answering, then describes his favorite as a brie-like cow cheese that is taller, rounder, and already gooey when cut. Because the transcript gives inconsistent renderings of the name, the row preserves that uncertainty rather than normalizing it from outside knowledge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=6712" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:52</a> | What are your thoughts on smoking cheeses? | He likes smoked cheeses and recommends using real smoke rather than relying mainly on liquid smoke. | He says smoked cheeses are good and describes exposing cheese to real smoke, such as from peat or sphagnum moss, while rotating it as it smokes slowly. He discourages leaning on liquid smoke and later says smoked Gouda works especially well in seafood enchiladas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=6946" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:46</a> | Could a brickmaker, metalworker, or fig knocker bully an average Egyptian farmer? | Usually not. He said those workers generally occupied different contexts, with metalworkers sequestered, brickmakers often enslaved, and fig knockers tied to seasonal orchard work. | He says those workers usually would not have enough contact with average farmers for that dynamic to arise. Metalworkers were sequestered, brickmakers were commonly enslaved, and fig knockers worked seasonally in orchards, so the relevant social and work settings normally did not overlap much. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=7033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:13</a> | What examples in Joshua suggest an early monarchic period? | He could not retrieve the specifics live, but said the structures of Joshua and Judges show similarities and that some material intimates an early monarchic setting. | He says he could not find the specific references live and would need to research them again. His limited answer is that some Joshua material seems to refer obliquely and retrospectively to monarchy, and that Joshua and Judges may fit an early monarchic period such as David or Solomon rather than a late monarchic setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=7450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:10</a> | Is your Ancient Egypt illustrated book suitable for a 9-year-old? | Yes. He said it has many pictures and hieroglyphs and can work for younger readers. | He says the book would be suitable for a 9-year-old because it includes many pictures and hieroglyphs. He adds that children have read his Ark of the Covenant book, and that picture-rich books help because people are visual learners and high-quality images draw readers in. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=7651" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:31</a> | What evidence supports Sinai being savannah-like at the time of the Exodus? | He pointed to mining-era evidence, rainfall patterns, vegetation needs, and broader environmental indicators that the region once supported far more pasture than today. | He points first to Serabit el-Khadim, arguing that copper smelting required large amounts of local wood and could not have depended on tiny imported loads. He also cites seasonal rainfall, wadis visible in Sinai, Old Kingdom depictions of Sahara wildlife, and the shift from cattle to caprids as drying progressed, all as evidence that the region was once much greener. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=8288" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:08</a> | What authority or responsibility did Adam have to confront the serpent in Eden? | He said Adam did have dominion and responsibility to protect the garden order, which means he should have resisted rather than passively allowing the encounter. | He says Adam had authority, power, and responsibility to care for God's creation, including his wife, and therefore should have rebuked the serpent. If Adam had resisted and prevented the fall, he speculates that humanity's provisional immortality would have become actual immortality through access to the tree of life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=8525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:05</a> | Could anyone move up in Egyptian society despite its sequestered structure? | Yes, but it was uncommon. He said some slaves could rise to high office or become scribes, while movement from farmer to merchant would be much harder. | He says Egyptian society was stratified but not absolutely closed; a slave could become free, enter scribal training, and even rise to high office such as vizier. He contrasts that with farmers, who were usually uneducated, so a farmer becoming a merchant would be harder than a skilled slave becoming a scribe. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=8789" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:29</a> | How can you use the marriage stele of Ramesses II if Kim Ryholt dates the hieroglyphic to the Persian period? | He rejected Ryholt's judgment here and said the stele can still be used because he considers that dating assessment unreliable. | He says Ryholt is often wrong in his judgment, especially in chronology and epigraphic matters, so he does not accept that assessment as decisive. He also says the marriage event is supported by other written material, including Hittite negotiations and inscriptional evidence, so a late-copy claim would not by itself invalidate the historical use. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=8943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:03</a> | What does Ryholt get wrong in his chronology of the Second Intermediate Period? | He said Ryholt gets the order of Dynasty 13, the interpretation of Dynasty 14, and the Abydos kingship wrong. | He says Ryholt's data aggregation can be useful, but he objects to Ryholt's interpretations. In particular, he says Ryholt's Dynasty 13 order runs against much of Egyptology, that Dynasty 14 should not be treated as the Abydos kings, and that the Delta was not united under a single Dynasty 14 monarchy. He adds that Ryholt's later work is better, but still carries some early eccentricities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=9266" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:26</a> | What is the difference between genealogy and ethnography? | He said genealogies explain descent and posterity, while ethnography is used to frame peoples and cultures in moral or social terms. | He distinguishes genealogy as descent or posterity, often focused on named individuals, from ethnography as cultural mapping or moral equivalence, often focused on nations and peoples. He notes that the two can overlap, such as passages that begin genealogically but shift into nations, so the categories require contextual judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=9756" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:36</a> | In the biblical sense, does `country` mean the same thing as `nation`? | He said there was not much difference, because political entities were nations in that context. | His answer is brief: in the biblical sense he does not see a major difference between country and nation, since political entities functioned as nations. The transcript does not expand the point much beyond that equivalence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=9786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:06</a> | Were the Exodus Hebrews the same linguistic grouping as the Amorites? | Yes. He said they were Amorites linguistically, culturally, and genetically in the broad sense he was using. | He says the Exodus Hebrews came from an Amorite city, spoke an Amorite language, and integrated readily with Amorite culture because they were Amorites in the broad linguistic, cultural, and genetic sense. He treats this as useful for explaining some later Hebrew and Egyptian language interactions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=9845" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:05</a> | What wine and cheese would you pair with Ultima 3? | He eventually suggested a Zinfandel, sourdough bread, and Camembert. | He first says he does not know what wine and cheese he would pair with Ultima 3, then settles on a Zinfandel, sourdough bread, and Camembert. The answer is playful, but he treats it as a real chat question and gives a concrete pairing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=9902" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:02</a> | Were Amorites Hebrews who rejected Israelite religion, or something else? | He said no; Hebrews were a splinter group from the broader Amorite world, while Amorites as a whole had their own religions. | He says the order is the reverse: Hebrews were Amorites, but a splinter from the broader Amorite world with a different religion. He uses Terah and Laban as examples of Amorites worshiping Amorite gods, while Abraham is culturally and genetically Amorite but religiously distinct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=10183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:43</a> | Who is a good underrated church father to study? | He recommended Clement the First and Chrysostom as rewarding early Christian writers to study. | He first recommends First Clement as a wonderful and under-studied early text. He then adds Ambrose for a post-Nicene option and Chrysostom, especially because he thinks Chrysostom dealt with issues still recognizable today. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=10328" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:08</a> | If James thought Philo was scripture, what does that mean about Philo's Logos theology? | He said `graphe` can simply mean writing, and even if an author is cited, that does not make everything the author wrote inspired or true. | He says James calling something `graphe` need not mean inspired scripture; it can simply mean that something was written or recorded. Therefore, even if James cites or alludes to Philo, that would not validate all of Philo's theology or make Philo's Logos theology authoritative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=10422" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:42</a> | Have you changed your opinion on translating Genesis 1:1? | No. He said he still rejects the introductory subordinate-clause translation and has not changed his position. | He says his interpretation may have shifted slightly over whether Genesis 1:1 functions as a summary or title, but not the translation itself. He still rejects the introductory subordinate-clause reading and expresses concern that similar arguments can misread temporal prepositional statements elsewhere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=10547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:47</a> | How does the clan view of genealogies explain Noah's ages around his sons and death? | He said the clan view treats the named figure as standing for a clan, so the long lifespan can describe the clan's duration rather than one individual life. | He explains that a clan can be treated literarily as a single person named for its progenitor or chief. On that view, Noah's hundreds of years can represent the life of the Noah clan, with `sons` possibly including later descendants, and the named ancestor receiving the clan's aggregate lifespan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=10689" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:09</a> | Is the church a visible institution like the Levitical priesthood and temple system? | He said there is visible order and practice, but not a one-to-one institutional parallel with the temple priesthood. | He says the church is a bit of both visible and invisible, using `mysterion` for a collection of parts that somehow functions as a whole. It has a visible presence on earth, but it is not a temple-like structure with walls, doors, and a consecrated priesthood administering ritual law. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=10959" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:39</a> | What is the relationship between the church and Israel, not just the modern country? | He described the church as connected to Israel's covenantal identity rather than as a detached replacement with no continuity. | He says Israel is the vine and Christians are branches, treating Israel as the source from which Christianity grew. He is speaking of Israelite religion rather than the modern state, and he describes Christianity as Israelite religion with Jesus as Messiah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=11046" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:06</a> | Do people misuse Romans 9:6-8? | Yes. He said the passage can be misused through anti-Semitic bias, but it still speaks of children of promise and Israel's eventual grafting back in. | He says the passage can be misused to support anti-Semitic bias, but that misuse is not what Paul is doing. He reads the immediate point as distinguishing children of promise from other Abrahamic lines, while also insisting Romans teaches that Israel will not be forsaken and will be grafted back in after the fullness of the Gentiles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=11282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:02</a> | Why do all interlinears use Strong's Concordance? | He said Strong's is used so often because it is public domain, free, and cheap, while better lexical tools are expensive. | He says publishers use Strong's because it is free, public domain, and cheap, not because it is the best lexical resource. He contrasts it with better but costly tools such as HALOT or Danker and says Strong's was already out of date when published, especially in light of later Koine Greek papyri evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=11814" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:54</a> | Is Papyrus font public domain? | No. He said it is not public domain and believes it is owned by Linotype. | He says Papyrus font is not public domain and believes Linotype owns it. He strongly discourages using it, calling it the Comic Sans of ancient-looking fonts, saying it does not look like actual papyrus painting, and describing it as ugly and overused. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=12025" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:25</a> | Is the Ezra in Nehemiah 8 the same Ezra from the book of Ezra? | Yes. He said the identification is straightforward. | He says it is the same Ezra and explains that Ezra and Nehemiah were originally one combined book, often called Ezra-Nehemiah in scholarship. Their later division into separate books does not imply a different Ezra in Nehemiah 8. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=12219" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:39</a> | Does Nehemiah 8:2 mean the event happened on a Sunday? | Not from that wording. He said `first day` means the first day of the seventh month, not necessarily the first day of the week. | He reads the phrase in context as the first day of the seventh month, a calendar date rather than a weekday label. He allows that it may have been Sunday, but says the text itself does not establish that. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=12300" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:00</a> | In 1 Kings 1:50, what does it mean that Adonijah took hold of the horns of the altar? | He explained the altar horns as part of the altar and said grasping them was a claim for sanctuary. | He explains that burnt-offering altars and incense altars had horn-like projections at the corners. Adonijah grasping them means he physically went to the altar and took hold of those projections as a sanctuary claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=12595" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:55</a> | Did Adonijah request Abishag because she had laid with David? | Yes. He said Abishag's connection to David made the request a political attempt to challenge Solomon's throne. | He says Abishag had not sexually lain with David, but she was attached to David as his final comfort wife. Because of that royal connection, Adonijah's request for her was a political gamble, effectively an attempt to undermine or overthrow Solomon's monarchy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=12736" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:16</a> | How did 1 Kings 2:27 fulfill prophecy? | He said Abiathar's removal fulfilled the word against Eli's house by ending that clan's hold on the priesthood. | He identifies the prophecy as the word against Eli's house in Shiloh. Solomon's dismissal of Abiathar removes Eli's clan from continuing to hold the priesthood, which he says is the fulfillment in view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=12830" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:50</a> | Why did Joab take hold of the horns of the altar in 1 Kings 2:28? | He said Joab was seeking sanctuary, assuming that killing him at the altar would desecrate it with human blood. | He says Joab was claiming sanctuary because killing a person on the altar would be treated as desecrating it with human blood, an unholy sacrifice. The strategy was to make people hesitate to kill him there, though he notes that determined rulers, including Solomon, still did kill people at the altar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=13032" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:12</a> | Does Solomon in 1 Kings 2:46 sound like a man of blood? | He said Solomon was eliminating rivals in a way kings commonly did, while `man of blood` more specifically describes a pattern of needless warfare like David's. | He says Solomon was securing the kingdom by removing rivals, a normal practice for ancient kings. In his distinction, `man of blood` is less about eliminating immediate threats to succession and more about a broader propensity for needless or prolonged warfare, which he associates with David's wars against the Philistines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=13390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:10</a> | If 1 Kings 3:2 says there was no house built for the Lord, did they still have the tabernacle? | Yes, but he said the tabernacle was diminished and incomplete because the Ark was no longer there. | He says the tabernacle was still at Shiloh, and some furniture such as the incense altar remained, but the Ark of the Covenant had been removed. On his reading, tabernacle worship was defective and not a unified whole, while the high places represented aberrant Yahweh worship rather than pagan worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=13663" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:43</a> | Is Hyperborea the same as Eden described by a different culture? | No. He described Hyperborea as occult fiction, not Eden. | He says Hyperborea is a fictional northern land imagined by an occultist, and jokes that it is probably Canada. His direct answer is that it is not Eden in another cultural form, but an idealized occult-fictional northern place. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=13810" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:10</a> | In 1 Kings 3:3, were Solomon's sacrifices to Yahweh or pagan gods? | They were to Yahweh, but in an unprescribed and aberrant high-place setting. | He says the sacrifices at this early point were not to pagan gods; Solomon's later apostasy is a different stage of his life. The problem in 1 Kings 3:3 is that the sacrifices and incense were offered to Yahweh in the wrong place and manner, on the high places. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=13895" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:35</a> | If Solomon was wise, why did he still fall into sin? | He said wisdom does not remove sinfulness; even wise people remain sinners and can make sinful choices. | He says wisdom and intelligence do not cancel sinfulness. Solomon could be wise and still sinful because sin is universal; smart and wise people may think they can get away with sin, but no one is above that condition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=14128" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:28</a> | What is the most accurate depiction of the Exodus in film or television? | He chose Cecil B. DeMille's version as the best of the available screen depictions, while still treating all dramatizations as limited. | He identifies Cecil B. DeMille's *The Ten Commandments* as the most accurate available depiction, especially the first part before the invented love story. He praises its handling of king, timeframe, culture, dancing, and games, but says the love story and the towering Red Sea parting should be cut or corrected. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QXhwWyCR6hc?t=14353" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:13</a> | Should Bible movies or series include ancient cultural context and more supernatural or spiritual elements? | Yes to ancient cultural context, but he said supernatural or spiritual elements should be used only where they actually fit the text. | He says Bible productions should include ancient cultural context, but should not overread events as supernatural or spiritual when the text does not require it. He distinguishes first-order miracles that defy natural law from second-order or hypernatural miracles, where natural events happen at the right place and time, and says many biblical miracles belong to that second category. |
